speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about how things had had had changed. Oh, this is tape number two, interview with Micheline Marmor. It's January 14th, 1997.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were talking about how-- how things had changed so much. And you-- you mentioned that that an Aryan administrator was put in charge of your parents'--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, instead of my-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with her at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I see her every day. I mean, she came every day to the business. That was her job. But can I finish the law? You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because after all these law were, I told you, very slow. And the two-- the two worse one were, you cannot go-- you cannot go out from the [INAUDIBLE]-- you cannot go out between the-- between 8 o'clock in the-- 8 o'clock in the morning-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no. 6 o'clock in the morning-- you can go out between 6 o'clock in the morning till 8 o'clock at night, in the afternoon. And that's all. You couldn't go out. Then, you couldn't go to the-- you couldn't go to the theater. You couldn't use a phone. You couldn't go to the restaurant. You couldn't go to the library. You couldn't go to park. And the people who has a job, like teacher, or for the government-- they couldn't-- they couldn't work anymore. And if you were a French officer [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the French-- in the French army, you didn't belong to the French army anymore. And the French considered them-- you know, they have been born there. Because before, they started by attacking all the foreigner or people-- but then they started with the French. Then the last-- the last law, which is the worst one, they thought, well, it's-- the Jew are not visible enough. It's not always written in your face. So they decided that you have to have a Jewish star, which is the Magen David. So you have to have, every-- every-- every Jew from the age of 6, has to wear this yellow star on their clothes-- I forget which side-- which have to be very securely sewn. And you cannot go out without.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they put on your identification paper, a big Jew, [FRENCH]. There is nothing-- we tried later on-- nothing you can do to take it out. And actually, this was the kiss of death. Because with-- if they ask your paper, and they see Jew, you are-- you call that deported? No. You know, you go to concentration camp. Let's call that deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I observed-- and it was very-- in a way, it was funny. Because the next day, all of the sudden, in Paris looked like the 4th of July-- star all over, the Jewish star all-- and people look at each other, like they didn't believe. Nobody knew who was Jewish or you were not. And-- and they were stunned. But of course, it was dangerous. So I observed people, the way they carried the star. And I didn't like it. They-- either they are scared, or they were ashamed, so they put something, you know, like a briefcase, or something like this. Or they bend their head. And I look at it. And I say, well, there is no shame to be Jewish. The shame should be on them, not on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a-- I remember, I had a very nice dark blue dress. And that-- that yellow star was on it. And decide, well, let's go. So I decide to put very bright yellow earring and yellow flower in my-- in my-- in my hair. So my mother look at me, say, where are you going like this? I say, I'm going-- I say, are you crazy? You want to be arrested? So I argue with her. So finally, I took off the flower. But as soon as I was in the street, I put them back. And I walk, you know, straight. I always walk straight during the war. And then the policeman saw me, and stopped me, and got really annoyed. He said, if you don't take off this flower, I will arrest you. So I guess I took off the flower. And this is my first act of underground, [LAUGHS] my first act of rebellion. And I have another experience, which was, one of my friend from school who was black, from the Martinique. And we bicycle. You know, in France, you-- very few people have car. My father had a car. So we bicycle. And in front of us, there was a [FRENCH]? A truck, full of German, you know, full of German soldier, looking at us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she look at me. And she say, you know, you are lucky. I am-- I cannot-- I am black, and I cannot hide it. But nobody see that you are Jewish. I thought that was interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it become-- it became very dangerous. They were arresting people all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a lot of, how do you call when you denounce people? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Stool pigeon? What's the word for-- Collaborators?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not collaborator, but people who give you, who give your name. [FRENCH]. Is that the one? [FRENCH], I think. There was quite a few. And my mother was terrified that-- and she was so scared for us, that she send my brother and I to sleep in the maid's room, in the sixth floor. And my brother will sleep half of the night and I will stay up listening. And then my brother-- I will wake up my brother, and he will stay, and I will sleep. And we did this for-- for few night. Then my mother say-- she got so worried that she decided to send us to Free France. So the-- she sent us to the woman in charge of the-- you know, the worker. She live in the banlieue outside of Paris. And she send my brother and I there. And from there, she pay somebody to take us from Ocuppy France-- it's called Occupy France to Free France, [FRENCH]. And I remember we went to the station. And I remember, it was really-- I never forgot, because when I-- it was very-- the worst place to be is the station. That's where the German-- because of course, everybody want to escape. That's where they pick. So I didn't take my Jewish card. I took my student card with me, you know, like a student. And then we went in the station. And inside the station, there was a-- like a school bus, a big bus. And all inside were a lot of-- full of people with sad eyes. And then I understood, they were arrested. And I look at it. And I say, I should be with them, you know. It was a very, very sad feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we got to the town, where we supposed to pass from-- I forget. I wrote it down. I [INAUDIBLE] the name of the-- and the man, who supposed to take care of us, say, well, I'm sorry. But you cannot pass in a boat, because the German kill people yesterday, who were-- and it's too dangerous. So we got very upset. I say, we cannot go back. We have to. We have to, to go to Free France. So finally, after we beg him, he say, well, if you want to cross by-- by feet. Right? That's what you say? By foot, by feet? Then I can arrange something. So all day, I look for a bathing suit [LAUGHS] in case. And then he-- so he told us that-- not-- the patrol. You call that the patrol? The people, who the German, who walk? Patrol.

speaker: interviewee
text: Patrol, OK. Patrol come and go. So he said, you-- I won't get to be in a boat. When I take my handkerchief, you run for your life. Because you don't have too much time. So but he said we didn't have to swim. And then I went with my brother, and a man-- very tall. And my-- both of them just go in the nude, you know, in the-- and then I-- and then the-- so we start. When we start-- when he showed that handkerchief, we start. You know, and then the man in front of me was so tall, he started to have water up to here. So I look at it. I say, well, I have to swim. We had to swim.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very difficult, because I had some sandal were bringing me down. So as I swim-- and then clothes. As I swim, I took off my shoes. And-- and it was very scary. And because it was so scary, I started to-- from nerve, I started to laugh. You know? You know what I mean, laugh? And my brother too. It was nerve. And we were lucky. We just got on the other side. How far was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- and there was a current. It's a good thing that we both knew how to swim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the water was? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it was in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what was the body of water where you were swimming? Was it a river?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was a river. Yeah. It was a river.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- who was it, just you and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and a man I didn't know. Oh, I know he was tall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your sister, and your mother, and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I forget to say. Oh, my-- my parents stay. They came later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I forget to say. My-- because my parents has American customer, one of-- one of them, who live in New York, gave-- you know, to come in this country, you have to have an affidavit. So she put an affidavit for one child. And because my sister was the oldest, then she went, in '39. So that was one person saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with my brother. So we got to Free France. And later on-- my parents came later on. You mentioned-- you mentioned the student card that you-- that you-- that you showed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, the student card didn't have a Jew on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And where-- where were you going to school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to the Beaux Arts, in Paris, you know? Here I was studying drawing, and anatomy, and painting. Later on, I study fresco. So what-- what happened? We went to Nice. Because Nice was occupied by the Italian.

speaker: interviewee
text: So thousand and thousand every day, we-- you know, it was like paradise. Because the Italian were wonderful. They were better than the French. Because-- not all the French, but the collaborator. Because the-- the French wanted to apply the German law.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Italian were in charge, and they didn't let them. You know, it was for the-- well, that was the collaborator government, you know, so it's the Vichy government. And if it was-- like, they wanted to deport, even children. But the Italian won't let them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, for about a year, the life was normal. My parents came. And the life was normal. I went back to another school, art school, which is called Arts Decoratifs. And I studied-- I studied there for, you know. And then life was normal. I used even to take my bicycle to Monte Carlo, which is close by. Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived Rue Shakespeare, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: An apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my parents. We lived in a-- actually, an army man, a commandant in the army. He was very nice. We lived there. But it was not dangerous, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- did your parents have difficulty getting out of Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was very complicated. They-- I don't remember exactly how. But I know that they were in a car, and with-- with some-- with some German soldier there, German officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- what year did they come? Do you remember what year it was, or how long you'd been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we are-- we are a few months after.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was in 1941? I think 1941 we went to-- '41, yeah. '41 to '42, I think. It was peaceful, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you-- when you got out of the-- when you got out of the water, when you were on your way, how did you-- did you get there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have to run.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the rest of the way? How did you get to Nice?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to run to the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- on foot?

speaker: interviewee
text: On foot, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was all on foot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was cold. [LAUGHS] I was worried that my brother catch cold. [LAUGHS] You know, he was the youngest, so. That was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the town was Nice? Was that Nice where you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Everybody know Nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's on the Riviera.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was all-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where-- well, there was a lot of place where it was occupied, you know, all this part. But where I live was Nice. So yeah, I told you, life was normal. And then from one day to another-- I think it's September '42. From day to another, the German came and occupied Nice. And the Italian left. And that was a-- that was a catastrophe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day, the next day, they started, what you call, [NON-ENGLISH]. They started to-- you know, to try to get as many Jew as-- and the terrible thing is most of the people who came live in hotel. Is that right? Hotel. And they all have paper like me, with Jew in it. So what they did is they go to every hotel. And they say, [POINTING] paper. And then they were you know, caught, and sent to deportation. First to a camp in-- I think in close to France-- close to Paris, transit. And then they were deported. So the way it all started, it-- I knew some young people from the youth hostel, when we go-- we went camping, and-- I knew some young people of my age and older. And they decided to do something, you know, not to just to be a sitting duck-- something, something like this. They decided to do something. So the first thing to-- and they-- they ask me if I want to join them. And you know, my parents were not too crazy, but they-- they let me. So I work with a man, which were-- who had confidant-- because I was really shy. And so he used to tell me, OK, you go to this hotel. And you ask all these paper who have these big, you know, Jew on the the-- on their identification paper-- ask them for two picture and their name, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we'll-- the first thing was to make false paper. Because one false paper save a life. With good paper, you were saved. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that's what I did. I went and get-- and then there was two people-- one, Pierre Muchnic, and I forget the other name. They were in a studio, somewhere in hiding. And that's all they did. They never came out. And in the beginning, they were not very good. You know, they were starting. But after, they became very, very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first thing, I have to change my name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I had very good paper. Like, my name became Micheline Beaumont. And the person I have the name-- actually, they was out of France. And you know, I have a completely different background. I was born, I think, in Grenoble, or something like this. And I have a baptise certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what you have to-- you have to have a card for everything. You have to have a card to eat-- food card, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] tobacco card. I didn't smoke, anyway. So all this little-- well first, the people who work got the best paper. But [FRENCH]-- that was-- that was my work, actually, and the work of all these people working with me. To save people means to find-- change their paper, find them a hiding place, and give them all these paper. And then all these young people went all-- all over the country to find hiding place. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Who-- who made the papers? How did you get the papers for your-- for yourself, I mean, starting with you? Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't make it. Whoever was in charge made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this-- in this-- in this photographic studio? Is that where it was made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well they were-- they started. They-- but they became very sophisticated. Like they-- around the [FRENCH], they could move the letter. And then we went to a-- you know, we have to get stamp, you know, what you apply. So we have to get stamp from all over the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the best place to get stamp-- if-- if a town has been bombarded, and was destroyed, that was very good. Because they couldn't check. And then a lot of-- I mean, a lot of French people were collaborator. But a lot helped-- a lot helped. Especially there was a lot of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you call? Minister, and priest, and people, even in the prefecture-- some of them were very willing to help. Well, another-- oh, then, so I change my name. All of a sudden, I don't-- I didn't want to meet anybody I knew. Because I was not Micheline Kaplan anymore. I was Micheline Beaumont. So one of my friend in school ask me if I have a place to-- to hide. And I said, no. She said, why don't you come and live with us? And that was very nice. I came and lived with her. Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- no, no, no. If not, it wouldn't have been any need. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So one day, everything was nice. One day, she told me, oh-- the mother told me, we know a family, and they need false paper. And they don't know where to get it. And they didn't know I was in the underground. I didn't want them to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it bother me, you know. I know I could do it. So finally, I went there. I say, you know, I know somebody who could make false paper. So I make false paper for them. And a few days later, they asked me to leave. Because it was too dangerous. In a way, I didn't blame them. I mean, if you hide a Jewish person, you could be deported with them. It was a very difficult thing to do. So you got the-- when you got these papers for these-- for the people, where-- how did you go about doing that? How did you get the papers? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so, when-- first we put them--

speaker: interviewee
text: For the people, when you were living--

speaker: interviewee
text: We put them in hiding. And I was the only one. I have quite a few family. And I was the only one to know where they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they hiding? Were did you put them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in apartment, in house, in a-- in a monastery, convent. And the most interesting place that we find was a leprosy. You know leprosy? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a terrible sickness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, leprosy! Leprosy.

speaker: interviewee
text: [FRENCH]. Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody will ever go there, especially not the German. We had some people there, and in-- in church, too. A lot of priest and minister help a lot. So my work--

speaker: interviewer
text: So then-- then who would do the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, they were in hiding. And who would do the-- who would do the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I go and ask them for a picture. And then first, we had a mailbox in somebody's house. But too much-- you know, we went there too often. It was too dangerous. So we find a better idea. There was a photographer in Nice, [FRENCH], actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a-- you know, how do you call? A business right there. And then to be safe, he took picture of all of us, because we came and bring the picture, and whatever we want. And then a few days later, we'll come back to pick up the false identity. So in case-- in case somebody was there, then I say, oh, I come to pick up my picture. And then he will have give us, you know, so there was no-- not to worry. He was finally deported. And he didn't come back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- Did he actually do the papers too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Oh, no. The thing is, the paper-- see, what was so dangerous-- anyway, I was lucky. I always went a little before or after the gestapo. But if I was there, not only I will I have been deported, but because these false paper were empty. So I will have been tortured. And that-- you know, that was terrible. I even tell-- they told you, never, never wear-- carry a list. You have to remember. Never-- and I told them, you know, if they torture me, maybe I will talk. [LAUGHS] I was not sure. I could have talk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So if you want an example of what we were doing is you know the Anne Frank, Well this woman who came, and bring them all kind of thing, and talked to them-- well, that's what I was doing, except I was much younger, and I was Jewish. That's the difference. And now, in that underground, there were some-- a few people were not Jewish. And why? There were so many-- nobody wanted to bother. First, it was dangerous. And some people didn't care.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The other thing is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I can give you-- well, sometimes they will send me to Grenoble, another part of France, where-- to get new stamp. So then I will go in the station, and-- and I bring back the stamp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's very dangerous if they-- so I will put it-- you know, in the-- in the station in France, you have the [INAUDIBLE], a net? And then you put your luggage. So then I will put this. And then if anything come, I will disappear. I will go to another compartment, like this didn't belong to me. How far did you have to go to get the stamp when you went to the other town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, quite far. The problem is, sometimes it takes so long, because the-- the underground will destroy the-- you know, the rail.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- when you-- how would you go about making contact in the other town to get the stamp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. We know. Everybody knew. I didn't know-- my underground, I knew the people there. But the man in charge-- Henri Pohoryles is one, and Maurice Cachou, who finally was caught in Paris. And he was-- unfortunate-- well, he became so-- he was so smart, that he went to France, and he was in charge of all the false paper in in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maurice Cachou That was the young-- I have his other name. And he was caught with a list, unfortunately. He was caught with a lot of my friends. And because he have a list, they torture him so much that he die under torture. They threw him in the-- [BACKROUND NOISE] I heard took the skin out. God knows what they did. It was horrible what they did. He was all bloody. I don't even want to describe it. With the other-- my friend were caught, that they put-- you know, they put your head in the-- in the water-- in the bathtub, till you can not breathe. And as soon as-- and then they bring you up. And then they beat you. And they torture you. And for the women, it was even worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know people who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who went through that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, half of the people who work with me never came back. Like, for example, we meet-- well, everything was done on the bicycle. You know, there was no-- there was no car anyway. But so we'll meet in a certain [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] street, [FRENCH]-- [FRENCH] like, you know, Columbus Circle, or something like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we change place all the time, so-- and then we bicycle, and we talk. We don't-- we don't stop too much. We talk, still moving. And-- but once-- once a day, we eating-- we all eat together. Somebody-- we pay somebody to cook. And we had one good meal together. Yeah, we've--

speaker: interviewee
text: And actually, we were-- we were not unhappy. You know, we were young. And we love life. And in a way, I could say-- for me, I could say, it was the best of time and it was the worst of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there at the end of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
